entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
Drawing on material culture, primary sources, and wider overviews, this course delves into the time period from 100 BC to 350 AD.

This topic is included in the following course(s): History: Grades 10-12 (U.S.), History: Grade 10 (Canada), History: Grades 11-12 (Canada)

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content: Inquisitive 9th graders could enjoy this course, but the main texts have an advanced reading level, so students would likely want to move at a slower pace.

rows.label: Ancient History: Grades 10-12
rows.scheduleInfo:
Ancient History: Grades 10-12
1 time/week 30 min
1 time/week 40 min
rows.books: Early Christian Writings: The Apostolic Fathers edited||Ancient Empires: From Mesopotamia to the Rise of Islam||Christians Reading Classics

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

books.title: Early Christian Writings: The Apostolic Fathers edited
Ancient Empires: From Mesopotamia to the Rise of Islam

blocks.text:
• Take a second to recall what you read previously in the same book or topic.

• If the previous passage is difficult to remember, try looking back at any chapter/section headings or illustrations or reading the first paragraph to jog the memory.

• Read the selection assigned OR read for the number of minutes allocated, leaving time for narration and mapwork. Use whichever guide (i.e., pages or minutes) works best for you. You want to read enough to be interesting, but not so much that you feel overwhelmed.

• If a book is challenging for you as a student, try stopping to narrate to yourself silently for a minute every now and then to make sure you are tracking. You might also want to read aloud to yourself. Or you might want to listen to an audiobook version of the text and read along with it. 

• If a book is an easy read, enjoy the ride! 

• Keep an eye out for quotes to put in your commonplace book or events or characters to put down in your Book of Centuries.

• Process and integrate your reading by narrating either orally or in writing. Some lesson plans will give you a suggestion of how best to narrate. 

• To keep yourself on track, you might want to set a timer, so you stop reading with enough time to narrate.

• After your narration, think about how what you read connects to other subjects, other books, and other things you've read, watched, or seen in the past or present. 

• Share any ideas that are particularly interesting to you with your fellow classmates, teachers, parents, friends, or others. You might even want to do a bit more reading or research on something on your own time in the afternoon or evenings.

These lessons are written directly to the student, but to assist them throughout the year, please read through this section and the lesson plans so you are familiar with the instructions and flow of the lessons. Accountability components are added to facilitate teacher/student discussion and growing independence. 

Those educating at home might pick one or two history lessons to read aloud with their students to keep up the habit of oral narration and foster conversation and relationship, and then simply give students these lesson plans and the rest of the books and check in with them throughout the term for conversation, support, and accountability. 

It is valuable for those in a classroom setting to also have a mix of lessons that students complete themselves silently and others read aloud and narrated together. Classroom settings lend themselves well to additional discussion and sharing at the end of lessons, after everyone has narrated either together or independently in writing.

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ancient Empires

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
Introduction: "What is an (Ancient) Empire?" p.1-9
Read for about 15 minutes and narrate. If you have already read this resource, just review the Introduction section assigned here.

Reading through the Introduction gives us an idea of the definitions, central theme, and theoretical model upon which the authors base the historical interpretations and ideas. Though there may be questions specific to the people, places, events, and times we are considering, the bigger questions help us frame the context for our studies. Note the IEMP model on p. 5.
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ STUDENT TIP
Use sticky notes to mark/note significant people, events, artifacts, etc. while reading to help you remember to put them in your Book of Centuries.

lessons.title: 40m Ancient History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: The Aeneid

➜ NOTE
In POETRY, you will be reading portions of the epic poem by Virgil, The Aeneid, Books 1, 2, 6, & 12. You will also have time to read some portions here. Read for the time allotted in either lesson time and pick up where you leave off.  

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Begin Book 1, p.47.  
Read for about 30 minutes and save the last 10 minutes or so for a quick narration. You can make a few notes, list characters or write a question, make a quick sketch, silently narrate, tell someone what is happening in the storyline, etc. Vary your narration types as you go.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• NOTE
An optional reading, Rubicon, is an engaging narrative covering this term's timeframe and topic. It does contain more mature themes at times and has some sensitive content. Please discuss with your parent if you choose to read this on your own.

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ancient Empires

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Introduction: "What is an (Ancient) Empire?" p.1-9
Read for about 15 minutes and narrate. If you have already read this resource, read The Aeneid, picking up where you left off.

Reading through the Introduction gives us an idea of the definitions, central theme, and theoretical model upon which the authors base the historical interpretations and ideas. Though there may be questions specific to the people, places, events, and times we are considering, the bigger questions help us frame the context for our studies. Note the IEMP model on p. 5.

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 13
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Links

➜ NOTE
Take both lessons this week to explore the ORBIS site.

➜ VIEW
∞ Link: Peutinger Table (Roman Road Map, 13th c. copy based on a possible Roman original)
Be sure to watch the YouTube clip to see the complete scan from west to east! A more detailed, interactive site where you can see individual sections is found on Euratlas. 
∞ Link: Euratlas

➜ VIEW
 ∞ Link: ORBIS
Experiment: with the ORBIS project to get a sense of how long it takes to travel by various methods across the Roman world. This is a Google Maps GPS for the Roman world! Click on "Start Exploring the Roman World", plug in your destination, your starting city, set your parameters (similar to avoid tolls, fastest/shortest routes), etc., and let the app give you your route info.

lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Early Christian Writings

➜ NOTE
Don't forget to read The Aeneid, Books 1, 2, 6, & 12 in POETRY.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.55-57 (Note the map on p. 54) "Ignatius of Antioch"
Read the background section on Ignatius. In term 3, you will read some of his writing.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITION
Docetism - a belief opposed as heresy in early Christianity that Christ only seemed to have a human body and to suffer and die on the cross (Merriam-Webster.com)
∞ Link: Vocabulary: Docetism

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 45
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Early Christian Writings

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.115-116 "Polycarp of Smyrna" (skip "The Epistle") & p.117-118 "The Martyrdom"
p.125-132 "The Martyrdom of Polycarp"
Narrate silently.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• TERMINOLOGY
The phrase "Away with the atheists" appears several times in some translations of the text but had a different meaning in Roman times. Roman persecutors called the Christians "atheists" because they refused to worship the Roman gods. Later, Polycarp says, "Away with the atheists" in irony, referring to the unbelieving Romans in the stadium.

⍞ Materials: Podcast link 

➜ PREPARATION FOR DIFFICULT CONTENT
This term, you will be reading martyrdom accounts where Christians are killed for their faith. Please discuss these readings with your teacher/parent.

➜ LISTEN
∞ Audio Link: The Forgotten Women of Church History
Listen to about ½ the podcast today, through the end of the story of Perpetua and Felicitas. 

NOTE: The podcast starts with a clip, and it sounds like you missed something—keep listening. The beginning sets up the purpose of the podcasts, and the interview starts about 5 minutes in.

➜ NARRATE
Begin a diary entry as if this story were happening today in your church.

⍞ Materials: Early Christian Writings

➜ NOTE
Don't forget to read The Aeneid, Books 1, 2, 6, & 12 in POETRY.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.60-67 Ignatius' Epistle "To the Ephesians" 
Be sure to read the background information on p.60.

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 65
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: The Aeneid

➜ NOTE
Don't forget to read at least once a week during your Poetry Lesson times.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Pick up where you left off.
Read for about 30 minutes and save the last 10 minutes or so for a narration. You can make a few notes, list characters, write a question, make a quick sketch, silently narrate, tell someone what is happening in the storyline, etc. Vary your narration types as you go.

lessons.title: 40m Ancient History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 66
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Link

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Link: Barbarians and Romans 
View a few of the artifacts across the top that interest you.
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ STUDENT TIP
Mark artifacts, events, people, publications, etc., for your Book of Centuries.

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 67
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Link

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Link: Justin Martyr's First Apology
Read for about 20 minutes and narrate. Read over 4 weeks.

lessons.title: 40m Ancient History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 68
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Links

➜ READ & VIEW
∞ Link: Eusebius on the Conversion of Constantine 
∞ Link: Nicene Creed/Niceno-Constantinopolitan Creed 

For more in-depth information, see: 
∞ Link: Council of Nicea (325 A.D.) AND 
∞ Link: Catholic Digest: A Short History of the Nicene Creed OR 
∞ Link: Zondervan Academic: The Nicene Creed: Where it came from and why it still matters

➜ NARRATE
List and elaborate on 5 things that stuck out to you while reading these documents.

lessons.title: 30m Ancient History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 69
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Link

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Link: Justin Martyr's First Apology
Read for about 20 minutes and narrate.

lessons.title: 40m Ancient History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 70
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Links

➜ READ & VIEW
∞ Link: Early Christian artwork 
∞ Link: The Church of the Nativity in Bethlehem
Be sure to click on the photo of the Church of the Nativity to see a gallery of pictures. 
∞ Link: Marble portrait head of the Emperor Constantine I 
∞ Link: Roman Catacombs

➜ NARRATE
Narrate orally and then add some artifacts to your Book of Centuries and/or make some sketches in your Architecture notebook.
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ STUDENT TIP
Mark artifacts, events, people, publications, etc., for your Book of Centuries.
